content.text: in React/Next.js + Claude, the best pattern is to stop rendering assistant output as one plain string and instead build a typed rendering pipeline around

, where each part can become markdown, code, files, tool results, reasoning, or custom structured data. In practice, a strong artifact system usually has three layers: a message-part router, specialized renderers for markdown/code/charts, and a safe schema that tells your UI when to render a chart or preview instead of just prose.

attrs.alt: Diagram for Building artifact renderers (code, markdown, charts)

content.text: The Vercel AI SDK chatbot docs explicitly recommend rendering

supports richer message types such as text, tool invocation, tool result, files, reasoning, and sources. That recommendation is the foundation for artifact rendering, because artifacts are really just
specialized presentations of structured message parts
rather than giant blobs of untyped text. If you keep your UI tied to
, your artifact system will become brittle as soon as you add charts, live previews, tool output, or multimodal responses.
A good architecture usually looks like this:
handles fenced code blocks or structured code artifacts.
renders structured chart specs or tool outputs.
provides a side-by-side expanded preview for larger outputs.

Do not try to infer everything from raw prose after the fact. If you want reliable artifact rendering, ask Claude to produce either structured message parts, tool outputs, or clearly delimited content formats that your frontend can recognize deterministically. In plain terms: free text is good for explanation, but artifacts should usually come from typed data, not regex guesses.
Your frontend should have a central renderer that maps message parts to UI components. The standard AI SDK pattern is already to loop over
, so artifact rendering becomes an extension of that idea rather than a separate subsystem.
A simple baseline looks like this:
This is the minimal shape, but in production you usually add custom data parts or tool-result renderers for charts, tables, and code previews.
Markdown is the most common artifact type because most Claude responses start as formatted prose with headings, lists, tables, and fenced code blocks. The AI SDK cookbook specifically recommends
and shows that markdown rendering can become expensive during streaming, since the content gets reparsed on every token update. Their recommended optimization is to split markdown into blocks with
and memoize block rendering so only changed blocks re-render.
That gives you two important production lessons:
Use a markdown renderer such as
Memoize parsed blocks for streaming performance.
A strong markdown renderer pattern is:
If the app streams long answers, the memoized block technique from the AI SDK cookbook is a much better default than reparsing the whole transcript on every chunk.

The cookbook warns that as chats get longer, naïve markdown rendering becomes exponentially worse because the full history re-renders during streaming. Their solution is block memoization plus optional

, which reduces update frequency and improves rendering stability. So for artifact-heavy chat UIs, performance is not optional architecture polish; it is part of correctness because laggy rendering makes artifacts feel broken.

Code artifacts need more than syntax coloring. A useful code renderer usually supports language labels, copy-to-clipboard, collapsible long blocks, line wrapping controls, and optional “Preview” or “Run” affordances for safe file types such as HTML, Markdown, or sandboxed React snippets. At minimum, if markdown code fences are embedded inside text parts, your markdown renderer should override the
component and route fenced blocks into a specialized UI.

<ReactMarkdown
  remarkPlugins={[remarkGfm]}
  components={{
    code(props) {
      const { className, children } = props;
      const language = className?.replace('language-', '') ?? 'text';

      return (
        <CodeBlockRenderer
          language={language}
          code={String(children).replace(/\n$/, '')}
        />
      );
    },
  }}
>
  {text}
</ReactMarkdown>
This turns ordinary Claude markdown into richer code artifacts without requiring a separate message format for every code snippet.
There are really two good strategies for code:
, best for explanations, snippets, and small examples.
, best for files, components, long code, or previewable outputs like HTML/JSX.
If Claude is generating something substantial, a structured artifact is better because your UI can attach metadata like filename, language, dependencies, preview type, and version state.
A useful artifact type might look like:
Then your renderer can decide whether to show plain code, rendered markdown, or a sandboxed iframe preview.

Charts should almost never be inferred from markdown tables alone. The better pattern is to have Claude return structured chart data through tool output, JSON schema, or custom data parts, then render that with your own chart components such as Recharts, Chart.js, or similar libraries. This is important because chart rendering depends on reliable typed fields like
, labels, and units, which prose does not guarantee.
A clean chart artifact schema looks like this:
maps that to a known chart component.
The best frontend pattern is usually:
Claude decides a chart is needed.
Claude calls a tool or emits structured JSON.
Your backend validates the chart schema.
Your frontend renders the chart from that validated object.
Claude also provides natural-language explanation alongside the chart.
This split matters because it keeps the

and streaming data parts, and the wider UI architecture supports structured transport beyond plain text. That makes custom data parts the most scalable pattern for artifacts like charts, tables, workflow cards, or file previews. Instead of embedding JSON inside markdown fences and reparsing it client-side, emit structured artifact payloads as dedicated UI data that the frontend can route directly.
Conceptually, you want the assistant stream to look like:
That produces much better UX than one giant markdown blob with a JSON appendix.

Large artifacts work better in a dedicated panel than inside the main transcript. Several artifact-oriented systems described in the search results emphasize a split-view layout where chat remains conversational and substantial outputs open in a side preview. In a Next.js app, a strong pattern is to show a compact inline summary in the message, plus an “Open artifact” action that expands the selected item into a right-hand drawer or panel.
Bigger surface area for code and charts.
Versioned iteration on a single artifact without flooding the timeline.

or mount arbitrary Claude-generated React code into your main app tree. If you support previews, use sandboxed rendering boundaries such as an iframe, strict allowlists, or a dedicated transform/runtime environment. For HTML previews especially, a sandboxed iframe is the safer default because it isolates styles and scripts from the host app.
React/JSX: only preview through a trusted compile/sandbox pipeline, not raw execution.
The AI SDK docs already show how to render

. This same pattern generalizes to downloadable artifacts like CSVs, PDFs, SVGs, and screenshots: if a file-like part comes back, use its media type and filename to choose the right preview or download UI. In other words, file rendering is another branch of artifact rendering, not a separate product feature.
A maintainable component tree often looks like this:
This structure keeps artifact logic composable instead of burying everything inside one giant markdown component.

If Claude is expected to generate artifacts often, define frontend-facing schemas explicitly. For example, for charts use Zod or a TypeScript type to validate the server payload before rendering, and for code artifacts require filename, language, and preview mode. The reason is simple: renderer failures are UI bugs, so the more you validate before rendering, the less likely Claude output is to crash your frontend.

Artifact renderers should degrade gracefully during streaming. For markdown, stream partial prose normally; for code, render once a full fenced block is available or show a “building code artifact…” placeholder; for charts, wait until the structured chart payload validates before mounting the chart and show a skeleton until then. This avoids flickery half-formed visualizations and broken parsers.
The AI SDK cookbook specifically recommends memoization of markdown blocks, and the chatbot docs expose

to reduce render frequency during streaming. For artifact-heavy UIs, this is especially useful because syntax highlighting, markdown parsing, and chart mounting are all more expensive than plain text rendering. A practical rule is: throttle stream updates slightly, memoize expensive artifact components, and only re-render the artifact whose source changed.
A more realistic renderer often looks like this:
The key idea is not the exact
shape but the separation of concerns: message transport, validation, and rendering each have their own place.

Artifact renderers should feel like tools, not decorative bubbles. Code wants monospaced density and utility actions; markdown wants typography and reading comfort; charts want clear labels, legends, and explanation; files want preview/download semantics. If every artifact is rendered inside the same generic rounded bubble, the UX usually feels cramped and indistinct.
Best practice summary in one rule
render prose as markdown, render code as code, render visualization from typed data, and never rely on one raw text string to do all three jobs
A strong concise explanation is: “For Claude artifact rendering in React/Next.js, I build on the AI SDK’s

. Text parts go through a memoized markdown renderer, fenced code blocks or structured code artifacts go through a dedicated code component with syntax highlighting and optional preview, and charts come from validated structured data or tool outputs rather than markdown parsing. That gives me safe, streaming-friendly, extensible renderers for markdown, code, files, and visual artifacts.”
